Using Firefox for Frontend Dev: Features and Advantages
People keep asking what browser I use and why it isn't Chrome — so here's a quick post on Firefox.
I've used Firefox for a long time. Logo is a fox curled around the planet, sometimes called "huoshou" (fire fox) in Chinese (
Worth admitting Firefox's market share in Taiwan is low — Chrome and Safari dominate here (Safari mostly because of iPhones):
In other countries it's a different story — Cuba sits around 14.09%, Germany around 11.59%. You'll hear "Firefox is dead, Chrome won" takes, but Firefox still has some genuinely nice features.
Firefox also has solid cross-platform coverage — Windows, Mac, Linux, Android, iOS, all supported.
If you're a frontend dev, you've definitely read MDN (MDN Web Docs). MDN is maintained by the Mozilla community — same group that makes Firefox.
If you're a frontend dev, you spend a lot of time in DevTools (right-click → Inspect in Chrome):
If English isn't your strong suit, Firefox's DevTools come fully localized — useful if you want native-language UI:
CSS Flex is the dominant layout technique now. Chrome added Flex support in later versions:
But Firefox's Flex tooling is more thorough — including a dedicated panel for Flex containers:
(Super useful for verifying your Flex syntax is doing what you think.)
CSS Grid is newer, and Firefox has supported visualizing Grid for a while:
(Personally I still use Flex more day-to-day.)
Sometimes you want to know whether a specific element has JavaScript event listeners on it, what type, and what code runs when fired. Firefox shows all that per element:
Accessibility work has a lot of detail to track. Firefox's a11y tools help — for instance, color blindness simulation. Here's what red-deficient color blindness looks like on my site:
Notice — my profile image looks much darker. Here's the original:

Chrome usually needs an extension to inspect or tweak fonts on a page — and even then you mostly have to type values manually. Firefox has font inspection and adjustment built in. Select an element, switch to the font panel, and tweak:
Chrome lets you inspect AJAX requests, sure — but sometimes you want to
modify an AJAX request and resend it
to test something. Chrome can't do that. Firefox has "Edit and Resend" built in:
Otherwise you'd need to reload the whole page to fire a new request. With Firefox's Edit and Resend, you fire it instantly:
Honestly, I just think these look cool.

Firefox has fewer extensions than Chrome — that's fair. But in real frontend work you'll keep multiple browsers open anyway. Whether you use Chrome or Firefox, as long as the tool fits your workflow, it's a good tool.

People keep asking what browser I use and why it isn't Chrome. So here's a quick post on Firefox's frontend dev features — better-than-Chrome Flex tooling, CSS Grid inspection, event listener inspection per element, accessibility tools like color blindness simulation, in-browser font tools, and an AJAX inspector that lets you edit and resend requests directly.
